It is difficult. Proof of this is the existence of numerous mathematical formulas—the South Korean formula, the European formula, or the most well-known one, the Swiss formula.
It doesn't really work because a product is not unitary.
It comprises numerous elements with rules originating from very different sources, and technically reducing customs duties is not as straightforward as one might think.
We are therefore in a technical deadlock, and politically, we attempt to emerge from this impasse through negotiations.

Now, it so happens that technologically, scientists have invented a new customs technology: the technology of deductible customs duties, deductible by the exporter from the economy of the importing state.
In practice, this refundable customs duty is transformed into a customs credit equal to the amount of customs duty paid by the exporter in the importing country.
This customs credit has three characteristics: it is refundable, negotiable, and redeemable.
It is refundable at the moment the exporter purchases from the importer, at which point they may deduct the duty they have paid.
It is negotiable because if the exporting company that has borne the customs duty center has nothing to purchase from the importer, it negotiates its customs credit on the stock market or at a bank.
And it is beneficial if we wish to assist developing countries' importers by enabling them to obtain a customs credit amount exceeding the amount of customs duties.
Well, with this technique, we create an international trade currency whose monetary base equals the existing amount of customs duties.
For example, the European Union would provide 13 billion euros in international currency within the commercial sector.

Mr. President, although the regulation on animal reporting has been in effect since January 2007, it appears that Member States are not systematically complying by submitting the annual reports requested of them.
Moreover, a comprehensive analysis remains unreliable, as many details are missing regarding the resources deployed to carry out inspections.
Member of an animal protection association and member of an animal rights advocacy group, this issue particularly concerns me.

Associations have been desperately fighting for decades, only finally achieving in 2007 the establishment of guidelines in this area, where it has become evident that Member States have shown a certain laxity.
I would even go so far as to speak of a certain unwillingness, for after all, we know that implementing controls and inspections is by no means insurmountable.
We know the slaughterhouses, we know the breeding sites, we know the livestock transit routes. So where is the problem?

I believe it is important in our time, when public opinion is increasingly, and rightly, concerned about animal welfare—whether in terms of breeding, slaughter, or transportation—that states respect these sentiments.

Since I have the floor, I would like to add that, regarding transportation, it would be essential to examine and take into account, independently of transportation times, the local climatic conditions.
Indeed, a several-hour journey carried out in the Netherlands during spring bears no comparison to the same duration of travel during peak summer in a country such as Greece.
Shouldn't nighttime transportation be imposed in these last cases?
I thank you for considering this proposal in the future.

In the past, the European Union has had to manage a number of health crises, which means that today we must be more vigilant regarding transparency in order to meet the expectations of farmers, as well as those of consumers.

The provisions outlined in this report have the advantage of imposing minimal costs on industries while delivering significant benefits to consumers, who are increasingly concerned about the quality of the products they purchase.

Adopting this report will help mitigate risks by ensuring better product quality, improved control, enhanced traceability, and greater information availability for the farmer, and ultimately for the consumer.
Today, with the growing volume of international exchanges, it is more than imperative to strengthen every preventive measure so that the food crises we have experienced in the past do not recur.

This report succeeds in reconciling the right to information, while strictly defining the nutritional elements that must necessarily appear on labels, with the equally important right to intellectual property for preserving the competitiveness of our industries.

I believe that manufacturers should indeed be required to immediately notify the use of any new raw materials entering the composition of animal feed, in order to ensure transparency and facilitate oversight by the competent authorities.
The inclusion of an emergency procedure enabling the addition of a new hazardous substance to the list of prohibited substances seems entirely indispensable to me.

Similarly, enabling the producer to question the competent national authority or the European Commission in case of doubt regarding an unfair claim helps better verify systems, protect the customer, and maintain the integrity of commercial transactions.

I therefore wish to fully support this excellent report, as experience shows that regulations on animal feed labeling—Mr. President, I conclude—prioritizing quality, transparency, traceability, and control, are the best guarantee for preventing further outbreaks of avian influenza in Europe.

Our Parliament continues to insist that this objective be applicable from the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon and that its details be clarified as soon as possible.

I wish, like the excellent report by Mr. Van Nistelroy, that territorial cohesion become the legal basis, ensuring the balanced development of all territories of the Union and enabling the optimal utilization of the specific characteristics of each European region.
It is essential to better coordinate community policies in order to maximize the impact of on-the-ground interventions.
Territorial cohesion is not intended to target exclusively regions suffering from permanent disadvantages.
It must nevertheless focus on the polycentric development of the European Union as a whole, taking into account the specific characteristics of each territory, while preserving their diversity.
This new concept appears relevant for addressing the outermost regions in order to ensure their sustainable and balanced growth.

In this regard, I thank the rapporteur for including in these compromise amendments my requests concerning the specific challenges faced by the outermost regions in terms of accessibility and competitiveness—essential elements of territorial cohesion.

recommendations regarding the improvement of governance in cohesion matters, thus making RUPs the first, albeit somewhat premature, illustration of the implementation of territorial cohesion.

Madam President, I believe that the question of distributing food supplies to the most disadvantaged is indeed timely; however, I think that, fundamentally, the Commission's proposal does not address the actual issue.

Sir, the issue of co-financing raises questions for me, and I believe it is rather the issue of liberalism that has guided our assessments of the CAP in recent years that should be called into question.
Riots against hunger and abrupt fluctuations in food prices are persistent indicators of the limits of agricultural market liberalization.
This liberalization has always come at the expense of vulnerable populations, vulnerable producers, and fragile territories.

I therefore advocate that policy should reclaim its rightful place, regain control at both the European and global levels, and ensure that the strategic domain of agriculture and human food, both in quantitative and qualitative terms, is not subject to the rigid laws of the market.
Certainly, there is urgency, and the Sigerski report responds to this urgency, for which I commend it; however, I wish that within the framework of the reflection now opening on the
It should be recalled that the primary objective of the CAP is to structurally combat hunger and poverty, which we experience in all Member States and worldwide.

Mr. President, during this period of economic recession, it was important that the European Union continue to demonstrate its solidarity with developing countries.
Let us recall that in 2007, the European community and its member states together provided 60 percent of total development aid, making the European Union the world's leading donor.
European Development Funds help combat poverty while promoting economic development and democracy.

I therefore welcome the fact that for the CFP countries, a budget of twenty-three billion euros has been allocated for the 2008–2013 period under the Tenth FED, which represents almost double the amount provided for the Ninth FED.
The audit of the accounts in its report concludes that the accounts of the seventh, eighth, and ninth FEDs are generally accurate, lawful, and regular.
One can only commend the record levels of contract implementation and payment execution for the Fs.
However, I also observe that further progress must be made to strengthen the control and monitoring system, as a significant level of errors continues to affect certain transactions.
Likewise, there is too often an excessive risk associated with financial support, and in my view, these risks should be better assessed.
On this matter, I share the view that budgetary support should only be granted if the recipient country has the capacity to manage the funds in a transparent, responsible, and efficient manner.

Among the priorities we must address in the future, I would like to highlight the integration of the debt into the general budget of the community, as this would enhance the effectiveness and transparency of development aid.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, of course, I would like to thank Commissioner Dimas both for his presence and for his responses, as well as for the attention he continues to give to all these disasters.

I think of maritime disasters, such as the Erika, and how many others there have been due to industrial accidents like Chernobyl.
There are other natural disasters besides fires.
I am thinking of the tsunami.
We may one day fear a tsunami in the Mediterranean, as there was one at the beginning of the 20th century that completely destroyed the city of Messina.

Mr. President, Commissioner, Ladies and Gentlemen Members of Parliament, once again this summer the Mediterranean forest has been ravaged by flames in Marseille, in Southern Corsica, as well as in Italy, Spain, and on a larger scale in Greece.
Over 400 hectares of forests and fields went up in flames, causing substantial material losses, irreversible damage to biodiversity, and dramatic loss of human life.
Appalled by this recurring assessment year after year and aware of the multiplying effect of climate change on wildfires.
I call for a European awareness to improve the coordination of efforts and the pooling of resources for fighting wildfires.
I wish for the realization of a large-scale Euro-Mediterranean cooperation project to combat forest fires within the framework of the Union for the Mediterranean.
I welcome existing initiatives, and in particular the European Forest Fire Information System, a pilot project.
I nonetheless call for a reform of the European Union Solidarity Fund so that it becomes a high-performing instrument for responding to crises and a reinforcement of European strength.

In my view, it is not realistic to admit at this stage that the Copenhagen summit is dead or that it will not lead to a comprehensive agreement ratified by all parties.
Let us not yield too readily to pessimism.
I advocate that at this stage, we should not lower our ambitions, either in terms of goals or deadlines.
We must continue to believe in a comprehensive, ambitious, and binding global political agreement, paving the way for the earliest possible conclusion of a legal treaty.
It is truly too early to discuss the deadline for COP 16 in December 2010.
We must now translate our rhetoric into genuine political will.
We must clarify our commitment in financial and developmental assistance to developing countries, particularly in terms of technology transfers.

I further note on paper the recent, exemplary initiatives taken by countries such as Brazil, South Korea, Indonesia, and Norway, which have specifically quantified their ambitions to reduce polluting emissions.

While one may understand the flexibility requested by the United States, which has only just begun to address climate issues, we must nevertheless achieve binding and ambitious quantified commitments from other major polluters, such as the United States and China, in the short, medium, and long term.

Madam President, we are all aware, and we have all stated, the importance of the Copenhagen negotiations; but while we all hope for an agreement, we cannot accept an agreement at any price.
If the conditions for an ambitious agreement are not met, I clearly wish for the European Union to be able to say no and refuse to sign a substandard agreement.
The European populations we represent here are awaiting an agreement, an agreement that would enable us to effectively combat the entire range of climate disruptions we observe around us every day.
They will go beyond mere announcements, non-specific statements of intent, and non-binding objectives that can be easily discarded once the media spotlight fades.

In Copenhagen, states will need to commit. The European Union—the only continent to have reduced its CO₂ emissions since 1990, and the only continent to have set precise, binding, and ambitious commitments for the future—cannot bear the burden of the effort alone.

This is why, if other industrialized and emerging countries are unwilling to assume their share of the burden, we must fully accept the consequences by imposing at our borders a tax safeguarding our industry against competition from those who choose not to participate in a global agreement.
We Europeans increasingly demand more and more from our industrialists every day.
For proof, I cite the climate package that was signed during the French pre-presidency.

For proof, I cite national initiatives, like the carbon tax, which were just praised by Mr. Pargnot and which I likewise welcome—these national initiatives drive the establishment of an ecological tax system.

A sector such as transportation, which is among the major emitters of CO2, contributes increasingly to combating climate change, even though certain modes of transport, such as aviation, emit fewer greenhouse gases.
It is a sector that has been severely affected by the repercussions of the crisis.

If, as I hope, maritime and air transport are included in the negotiations in Copenhagen, then let us ensure that any demands placed on European industries are also placed on those of other industrialized countries.
In Copenhagen, world public opinion will be focused on the leading actors. Please conclude, if you please. Do not let us down. Thank you.

Madam President, Mr. President of the Council, Mr. President of the Commission, dear colleagues, with less than two weeks remaining until the Copenhagen conference, concerns are growing regarding the prospects for success in climate change negotiations.
Some others already claim that it will be a failure.
Major global powers still hesitate to demonstrate the full ambition that the climate emergency demands.

By adopting tomorrow the resolution proposal on the European Union's strategy ahead of the Copenhagen Conference, we, European parliamentarians, must demonstrate that our determination is absolute, and that the Conference must lead to an agreement that is not only precise, but above all binding.
Moreover, the binding nature of this agreement, which will be deemed established, must not be subject to challenge or dispute.

One cannot indefinitely postpone decision-making until tomorrow, otherwise it will be too late; hence the necessary creation of a global environmental organization, which today appears not only essential but also urgent, as it will be established under the auspices of the United Nations to oversee the implementation of commitments made in Copenhagen.
From Copenhagen onwards, we must be able to expect from the world's major stakeholders greater genuine commitment and responsibility.

The Copenhagen summit appeared to the world as a unique opportunity for global leaders to bear witness, take action, and address the consequences of the process known as climate change.

The European Union was at the forefront of this platform, not only due to the bold legislation we have in place regarding the limitation of our activities' impact on the climate, but also because Europe represents an alliance of countries that can truly make a difference.
Certainly, the outcome of the Copenhagen conference can only leave a taste of dissatisfaction, but I refuse to speak of failure.
The world has reached agreement on the issue of limiting temperature increases to 2 degrees, even among countries previously most reluctant, such as China.
Brazil has just enacted a law aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emissions by nearly thirty-nine percent by 2020, in accordance with its commitments.
Many others have also demonstrated significant ambitions regarding greenhouse gas emission reduction targets.
I am thinking of Japan and Norway.
Moreover, it is also necessary to highlight this very positive aspect: the consensus reached regarding the urgent need for a mechanism to reduce emissions caused by deforestation.
A specific and unanimous text has been established regarding the implementation of the direct mechanism to reduce deforestation and thereby enable natural CO2 storage.
Far from a binding agreement, but clearly in line with a political understanding, the outcome of Copenhagen must lead us to reflect on the reasons for such disappointment.
I attended the Copenhagen Conference, like many of my colleagues, with the intention of participating, contributing, and witnessing a negotiating Europe, a leading Europe in action.

To assert itself, Europe must demonstrate greater strength on this issue.

I now give the floor to my esteemed colleagues. To move beyond caricatures—some of which we have already heard this morning—I would like to recall a few objective facts regarding Tunisia, a partner country of the European Union.

Social conditions have evolved very favorably, marked by a spectacular increase in average income per capita, and now the vast majority of Tunisians meet the criteria defining membership in the middle class.

Over ninety percent of Tunisians benefit from social coverage, and more than a quarter of Tunisia's budget is allocated to education, so that the country ranks among the first to have achieved the Millennium Development Goals in primary education.
Finally, Michel reminded us just now, women's rights are recognized and guaranteed.
At the university, there are more female students than male students, and 40 of the professors are women. One quarter of the deputies, local officials, or journalists are women.
Many countries could envy such results.

is clearer for businesses to provide them with a single, comprehensive response that will enable them to reconcile the goal of simplifying obligations desired by all with the realities of small businesses that need it.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, and dear colleagues, as my colleague Elisabeth Morin-Chartier and other colleagues have indicated, on the 27th and 28th of February last, the violent storm Cynthia struck several regions of France, resulting in fifty-three fatalities and extensive material damage.
The storm was exceptionally intense, causing devastating floods along the French coastline.
After Mayor, Europe is once again affected.
Brittany, the region of my colleague and friend Alain Cadec, was severely affected.
Three French departments were thus declared to be in a state of natural disaster: Brittany, Poitou-Charentes, and the Pays de la Loire.
Faced with these disasters, the European Union must demonstrate its responsiveness and express its solidarity.
It is not only through words and a resolution, but also—and above all—through financial assistance that we will be able to support the victims of these disasters.

I therefore invite, and my colleague Christophe Bechus joins me in this appeal: the European Commission has swiftly released funds from the Union's Solidarity Fund to assist the affected regions in coping with the damage.
This disaster underscores the relevance of Michel Barnier's proposal to establish a European civilian intervention force.

Europeans must act together, as disasters are increasing in frequency and affecting our European territories, particularly our most isolated and vulnerable regions, such as the islands in the Indian Ocean and the Caribbean.

Madam President, I too would like to congratulate our rapporteur, Jörg Leirich, on his report and his spirit of cooperation on this matter, and I particularly support his proposals regarding the funding of aviation security, since we are discussing aviation security, not aviation safety.
It has been many years now that our Commission has been advocating the principle that stricter security measures must be borne by the Member States, and I fully support our rapporteur on this matter.
Indeed, security concerns not only travelers but all citizens, and it is the responsibility of states to take measures to combat terrorism.
It is therefore unjustified that air passengers alone should bear the cost of security measures at airports and on board aircraft.
And this is all the more true given that, in transport modes such as rail and maritime, states partially or fully fund security measures, depending on the case.
I believe we must remain firm before the Council and defend to the very end Amendment Thirty-Two of the report adopted by our Transport Committee.
I had another point to address briefly.

While I support the progress made in the areas of non-discrimination and transparency that this text brings, I do not believe the objective of this directive is to establish a single, uniform European system of security charges.
While ensuring compliance with common rules, Member States should be allowed to retain their current system of pre-collection of fees.

Thus, in countries such as Spain, Italy, or France, where levies are approved by Parliament, it must be the judicial authority responsible for reviewing laws that serves as the independent supervisory body.
This is the purpose of Amendment 40, which I hope many of you will support when we vote on it.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, the report I am presenting to Parliament seeks to explain that, in the fight against climate change, agriculture has a role to play and a place to occupy.
This week, nature reminds us that volcanoes, in particular, can have a major impact on human activities and on the climate.
But this nevertheless requires that men and Europe, in particular, take their responsibilities in combating what is known as global warming.
My report attempts to demonstrate that agriculture can simultaneously continue reducing its carbon emissions, decrease its consumption of fossil energy, and above all, from another perspective
On the side, actively contributing to carbon fixation to globally reduce carbon emissions and increase the amount of carbon fixed and sequestered.
This report attempts to show that in agriculture, we should move away from an approach—how shall I put it?—fragmented, whereby whenever we encounter a problem, we simply open a new directive.
on the soil, on water, on pesticides, and who knows what else, and to attempt to address the agricultural issue with the ambition of tackling it in a comprehensive, systemic manner, and

In this approach, one must be able to identify pathways to ensure that agriculture, as I mentioned earlier, not only reduces its energy consumption but also actively contributes to carbon sequestration.
It is particularly the role related to photosynthesis and biomass, and above all what matters most to me, linked to soil and the capacity of European soils to sequester organic carbon.

I believe this path I am opening in this report—and I hope it will be followed during the vote to be held in plenary in Brussels—must enable European agriculture to enter a phase of sustainability, to ensure

ecological transition towards an agriculture that would position it ahead of numerous other continents or agricultural systems, ensuring it is economically, ecologically, and, I believe, socially effective from that point onward.

This report addresses a specific issue—climate change mitigation and agriculture—but it also aims, I hope, to open up other perspectives, particularly to contribute to the debate surrounding the future of agricultural policy after 2013.
I am addressing the Commission; I have frequently addressed and will continue to address the Agricultural Commission; I will also address the various ministers whenever the opportunity arises.
I think we need to make a turn.
This 2013 pact must be ambitious.
It must be based on objectives that enable citizens to understand why we need a common agricultural policy.
We need a common agricultural policy because Europe's agriculture must ensure food security, while also committing to sustainability.
This is at least what I propose in this report, and this is what I hope the Parliament, by a large majority, will follow.

Mr. President, I would first like to emphasize that the precautionary principle implemented by most European governments—closing their airspace in a targeted and temporary manner—was a wise and prudent decision.

The safety of our fellow citizens must take precedence over any other consideration, and under these circumstances, the stance of certain airlines demanding the immediate and total reopening of airspace, even for just one or two test flights, appears at the very least indecent.

I believe it was Mr. Heéain earlier who spoke about the complementarity of transport modes, particularly with regard to trains, and I shall take this opportunity to express how much I regret the air transport paralysis.
has been exacerbated in certain countries such as France by the disruption of railway transport due to strikes under these irresponsible and incomprehensible circumstances.
Returning to our subject, I wish to welcome the Commission's decision to authorize the granting of public aid to airline companies affected by the ongoing disruptions.
It is a sensible decision in a context already marked by crisis, but its support measures must be exceptional in nature.
In this regard, I wish that among the criteria used to grant them, the exemplary conduct demonstrated by the airlines in compensating customers affected by flight cancellations be included.
Indeed, it is unacceptable for certain companies to abuse the force majeure clause in order to evade their obligation to compensate passengers under Regulation No. 261/2012.
Travelers are victims of the current situation, and if no alternative solution is offered to them, they should not, in addition, be made to bear the financial cost.
Furthermore, travel agencies are not required to refund unused flights to travelers.
And this is not normal either.

Companies such as travel agencies have insurance coverage that protects them in exceptional circumstances, such as the one we have been experiencing over the past few days, and it is therefore essential to ensure that travelers are properly compensated for flights that have been canceled.

knowing that 750 European passengers have been affected, with a significant number still stranded in various corners of the world. Knowing that economic losses grow exponentially over time, how can one explain that it took so long?

secure, with unchanged meteorological conditions, and given that the volcano remains active, one may reasonably question what additional safety assurances for passengers we could not have provided sooner.

coordination of other transport means under exceptional circumstances, but let us also take into account the necessary coordination of assistance for the tens of thousands of stranded passengers, who likewise have the right to information and support.

Mr. President, High Representative, this attack on a civilian vessel by Israeli commandos undoubtedly requires an impartial and credible investigation.

This attack occurred in international waters, thus involving the international community, particularly the European countries bordering the Mediterranean.
Europe, which should speak out more forcefully and become more actively involved in seeking a political solution based on two states, each with Jerusalem as its capital.

Brutal attack on the Mavi Marmara by the Israeli army sparks emotions and condemnation
Emotion upon seeing the nine victims of the civilian assault, condemnation because this reasoning in international waters constitutes a violation of international law.
In doing so, the Israeli government penalizes its own people, as ultimately, with the strengthening of extremist factions, the ultimate goal of security in the region recedes further.

Madam President, Madam High Representative, I congratulate the new momentum generated by the launch of the Partnership for Modernization and its pragmatism.
This new partnership, if nothing else, would at least underscore just how indispensable Russia remains as a geopolitical partner for the European Union.
I also commend the positive remarks regarding cooperation with Russia on crisis management.
A few remarks, however.

While the requirements of the Union are consistently emphasized, the interests or positions of its partner appear systematically underestimated or dismissed, whether in the field of energy or in how it assesses the Eastern Partnership and the Atlanticist aspirations of its closest neighbors.

I also note that the firmness displayed by most Member States and the Union in their statements, as well as the severity of condemnations of human rights violations, appears to be reserved exclusively for Russia.

The remarks are more restrained, diplomacy more muted, as if referring to China, for instance, to these systematic violations of the rule of law—consider, for example, the Laogai camps, this global factory in light of minimal European social standards, when our French or European workers are unemployed?

I remind you of the adage from d'Agusta, father Augusta: to reach the peaks by narrow paths, for this is how I view Russia's current efforts, freed from the criminal poison of Marxism, which for so many decades drained and imprisoned this land.
The current Russian Federation has managed to lift the head of an entire nation, despite the significant challenges and setbacks that still lie ahead.

Thus, a little humility, gentlemen of European integration, especially when the proposed European superstate fails to achieve any of its ambitious goals despite increasingly costly resources and the growing dominance of the states of Western, Central, and Eastern Europe.
I cannot help but recall that this assembly showed greater indulgence toward the more communist Soviet Union than toward Russia.

Indeed, they are often the very same individuals who yesterday campaigned for the unilateral disarmament of the West and for participation in Mr. Gorbachev's "common house," and who now experience the most virtuous indignation and the greatest embarrassment regarding the Russia-EU partnership.

On this subject, I must inform you—should you not yet be aware—that new historical phases are emerging as a result of the gradual translation of Soviet secret archives, which the diligent Mr. Pavel Stroïlov has managed to obtain. These archives have already yielded and will continue to yield significant insights.

Unpleasant surprises regarding the conventional wisdom on the reality of the end of the Cold War, which was genuinely Mikhail Gorbachev, and the betrayal by today's European powers, who at the time enthusiastically sought to enable certain Soviet projects.
At the risk of repeating myself, Russia is an essential and strategic partner for European states, and our relations must be strengthened for the benefit of all parties.

This country is far closer to us in terms of culture, civilization, history, geography, mutual interests, or shared risks than certain states you are nonetheless preparing to admit to the European Union.
President of the Council, it is often said that necessity makes law.
Faced with the crisis, one truth stands out: the necessity of innovating, of being creative—a necessity now impossible to ignore.

In this regard, I welcome the progress made by the Council—a step, which I hope in time will become a giant leap, toward genuine economic governance, for let us not be mistaken: mere coordination of economic policies will remain insufficient.
May we achieve a genuine budgetary federation, not imposed by circumstances, but because we have collectively decided upon it ourselves.
Let us return to the council's decision, which still pleases me.
Strengthening the preventive component of the Stability and Growth Pact is a positive development, but there can be no genuine budgetary discipline without sanctions for those who deviate from it.
They have been strengthened, but not yet clarified—this is regrettable.

Otherwise, I will express my satisfaction, among other things, regarding the decisions taken, such as the famous competitiveness assessment dashboard, since that is precisely what this is about—reconnecting with competitiveness and sustainable growth for all.
Hi again on the project to establish a banking tax, the acceptance of taxing financial transactions, the acceleration of financial regulation reform.
Mr. President of the Council, all these decisions and guidelines seemed a few months ago, indeed even just a few weeks ago, unrealistic, illusory, or truly hypothetical.
You're right, it's true, it represents a profound cultural shift at the level of policy for small European passes.
That said, it is now absolutely essential that, in October, states implement these guidelines in a very concrete manner.

It will now be up to the Council whether this step taken becomes a historic leap forward or merely an empty wish, something we will all bitterly regret, and which will particularly burden the majority of citizens who will have to live with its consequences every day.

Mr. President, we have already discussed this matter during the Presidents' Conference. That is why we requested to postpone the debate and vote on the Gallop report, namely until September, when the Conference of Presidents will have taken place.

Since the fruit is not yet ripe, let it ripen over the summer and let's discuss it in September.

Madam President, I must admit I find it difficult to keep up with my colleagues, who so readily display their strength and claim the monopoly of our legitimacy vis-à-vis the other institutions of the Union.
Unfortunately, their stance is often laughable.
But when the members of this household, through hard work and conviction, assert a well-reasoned position when
We defend what resembles a European general interest against rigid states; the situation changes entirely around us.
In this debate on European supervision, the Parliament has played this role.
Regarding the European Systemic Risk Board, of which I am the shadow rapporteur for my group, I have advocated, like rapporteur Ms Goulard, an ambitious approach.
I have examined mechanisms ensuring a certain level of efficiency, and I have emphasized the paramount importance of the European Central Bank.
Why?
Because the European Central Bank is indeed the only community institution within this framework, and because it has demonstrated its seriousness.
Out of Christian charity, I shall refrain from mentioning the names of all the institutions and high-ranking individuals who suddenly vanished during the storm.
We can consider ourselves fortunate to have had at least one captain on board during the height of the crisis.

I was therefore reassured to see that tomorrow's macroprudential supervision would be anchored in a strong community institution, whereas microprudential supervision would inevitably remain fragmented.
In certain troubled circumstances, the European Systemic Risk Board must get to work as quickly as possible.
The future will show whether adjustments will be necessary, but it is essential to start on sound foundations, as systemic risks affect the entire banking sector, both in Europe and worldwide.
Mr. President, the final agreement reached on the revision of the directives concerning industrial emissions is more than acceptable, and I commend the work of compromise that has been accomplished.

This final package incorporates the key positioning points I have advocated, together with several of my colleagues on the Environment Committee, aiming to make this text an ambitious yet realistic piece of legislation.
First, maintaining a certain degree of flexibility for businesses and competent authorities in granting exemptions.
Next, I would like to welcome the new version of the NOS, so controversial and now limited to certain sectors, which appears much more achievable.

It is also important to emphasize that the agreement reached enables better alignment with the ETS directive and definitively removes from the text of the new directive the largely irrelevant notion of establishing a market for SO2 and NOX allowances.
A few caveats to keep in mind, however, particularly those concerning the soil.
Since the SOL Directive remains pending, this important issue will unfortunately be addressed only by those Member States that have their own legislation in place.
Tomorrow, Parliament must adopt the elements of the agreement reached following the trilogues.

Enormous effort has been made from all sides to avoid sending this text back for conciliation—a text which, I must emphasize, is of crucial importance both for industry and for European environmental policy.

As for the European Union, I believe it is necessary for it to proactively establish measures to combat this disaster, and I think this is obvious.

who announced having invested in Brazil, quoting, and this is in his report, using this country as an export platform for Europe.

Europe, without wishing to pursue its policy of offshoring by intensifying its attacks.

Mr. President, many colleagues are concerned about the potential consequences for European agriculture resulting from the reopening of negotiations on an association agreement with Mercosur.
My group and I share these concerns.

It is indeed a concern, and as a Member of Parliament representing an ultraperipheral region, I have firsthand experience with banana agreements. It is therefore a concern that an agreement with Mercosur could impose difficult costs on numerous European agricultural sectors.
This is why an impact assessment is absolutely necessary.
It is also feared that this agreement may further steer Mercosur's agriculture too heavily toward a productive model, to the detriment of the small-scale farms that exist in these countries.
A balance must be struck so that this agreement does not turn Latin America and Europe into a superpower essentially grounded in the dogma of mercantilism. Mr. President, thank you.
Mr. President, I would simply like to say that we may not have properly set today's agenda.

If we allow free trade agreements to bring products into the territory of Europe that do not comply with either our social standards or our environmental standards, then the reform of the CAP will be pointless.
Very clearly, we cannot remain in hypocrisy, or else we impose environmental, sanitary traceability, and safety constraints on one hand.
To our farmers, on the grounds that consumers must be protected, and on the other hand, allowing products to enter our continent freely without regard for standards.
The question underlying the negotiation with Mercosur is indeed that one.

The way things were initiated, regardless of what we are told about the Parliament having been very well informed, and about matters having proceeded with the greatest transparency and clarity, does not lead us to view favorably a negotiation that takes place at a time when, in parallel, talks continue at the WTO.
On the grounds of consistency in defending consumers and defending our farmers, I do not believe we should continue along this path.

Madam President, I would also like to commend the work carried out by rapporteur José Boveis, to acknowledge the Commissioner, and to refer to the intervention of Mr. Deès, who preceded me, on the objectives of this report.

I believe it is very important that we clearly keep in mind that in the debate we are having, we are addressing a problem related to price volatility in agriculture and especially to the fact that when prices
They quickly affect distribution prices, but when prices fall at the production level, there is no corresponding effect on consumers.
There is indeed an organizational problem within the supply chain, and there is indeed a problem regarding the distribution of what is called added value within the supply chain.

I believe José Bové's report offers, on several points, perspectives that must be highlighted, and I am addressing this directly to the Commission in an effort to break out of this fundamentally negative spiral.
The first, of course, is the organization of the supply chain and the power dynamics within the supply chain. Therefore
What proposals do we put forward to enable producers to come together, organize, and thereby gain greater influence within the supply chain for negotiation purposes?
That is the question you raised, Mr. Commissioner, regarding interprofessional bodies, but it is also a question that arises from the contractual framework that will be established.
I argue here in the report that this contractual basis is a European framework.
One cannot allow each country to solve this problem on its own.
There must be a European framework.
It must be flexible, but it must exist.
It is a proposal, an inseparable path for me.
I am, of course, fully aware of the issue of price transparency.
And on this point, I support the establishment of this observatory, which is absolutely necessary in order to inform consumers about the evolution of prices.
Here is my support for the proposals that have been made, and I hope, as Mr. Des mentioned, that there will be a majority in favor.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, today I wish to welcome this debate, which once again underscores the need for the Union to assess and value its regional diversity.
At a time when negotiations for the new framework programmes 2014 are underway, it seemed to me all the more necessary to emphasize the specific arrangements that certain regions should benefit from.
Questions of territorial continuity and cohesion must be at the heart of our concerns.
But the development of mountainous, insular, or sparsely populated territories requires more than a set of derogatory measures.
We need a horizontal approach.
We must establish the tools capable of synergistically integrating all areas of community action in order to strengthen their respective added values.
Moreover, as a representative of the ultraperipheral regions, I am awaiting, and patiently so, the new strategy of the Union for the UPRs that the Commission's services promised us for early 2011.
