As the next summit approaches on October 6, China and the European Union must absolutely and firmly strive together to make progress on the issue of climate change.
Last year, at the same time, we were already engaged in this very hemicycle, urging Europe to do everything possible to achieve an ambitious and binding agreement in Copenhagen.
It has been observed that the outcome fell short of our expectations and aspirations.
Thus, concerns one year later are the same, or even much more intense.

In order to be able to constrain or persuade our partners today, we must demonstrate that our determination remains absolutely intact, for example by ensuring we have the means to fulfill our commitments to Southern countries.
To this end, the establishment of innovative financing mechanisms has become a necessary imperative—and I heard President Sarkozy's speech at the UN yesterday.
For instance, the introduction of a carbon border tax at our borders cannot be ruled out.

It would act as an incentive mechanism for countries that are not going far enough in their climate commitments, and far more than a mere threat, serving as a means to protect our jobs and businesses, particularly in better preparing for the pre-crisis phase.

Madam President, in my view, the need to enhance family leave is not justified. Raising the minimum threshold for maternity leave is a positive step and a benefit, and we should not be entirely populist in comparing economic impacts with qualitative benefits that are difficult to quantify.

But the issue is essentially twofold: first, the economic context, which is indeed real, but insufficient to leave millions of families stranded for decades. Second, the legal gaps in the report, as the text includes several types of family leave whose legal foundations are incompatible.
Consider the adoption leave provided for in the text, on an equal footing with maternity and paternity leave.
Personally, as an adoptive mother and on behalf of all those I represent, I appreciate the intention to grant rights equivalent to those of biological parents.
I am in fact the additional small package mentioned by Mrs. Morin, which was added to the EtréLA report.

If the objective is to ensure health and rights for all women in the labor market, adoptive women, having become mothers in the same way as others, are entitled to the same rights and the same protection at work.
They are mothers just like any other, whether they adopt a child under or over twelve months old—no discrimination, please, as stated in the text.
With regard to adoption, I regret that the text has not been examined in sufficient depth.
It was not even fed by the Rambo impact study.
None of this is particularly well-developed, which is clearly a weakness.

Nevertheless, despite these reservations, I will support Mrs. Estrella's report, as beyond economic considerations, there are men and women who must not only better assume their parental responsibilities in a society of

Increasingly disengaged from the education of these young people, yet moreover, it is our duty to ensure that no one has to choose between sacrificing their children for their work or sacrificing their work for their children.
Finally, we do not sit on the Council but in Parliament. If we direct electees are not ambitious, tell me who will be.
Madam President, Commissioner, President of the Council, I would like to intervene, without revisiting the debate as it has unfolded so far.

We discussed the 2011 budget under the constraints we are aware of, namely those arising from the deep crisis currently affecting Europe, with public deficits at the national level, which may lead to two hypotheses.
These national-level public deficits constrain the European deficit, or these national-level deficits must provide a new direction and renewed momentum at the European level.
That is precisely the question.
I ask you, Mr. Commissioner, above all to try to think when discussing a budgetary revision, when we begin to talk about budgetary perspectives for after 2013, not to
remain completely immersed in the context of the crisis, unless you are telling me and telling all the citizens of Europe that, definitively, Europe will remain in crisis for a long time.
Our main challenge is indeed managing the crisis and reducing our deficits and debt, but it is also about preparing for exit from the crisis.
And it is there that the budgetary question arises.

We will not be able to overcome the crisis, as Joseph Dale stated immediately, unless the European budget, because it is European, is capable of providing us with the means to regain today what we lack in terms of growth, job creation, innovation, and research.

That is the issue at stake, and it is to this honor that we must address the budget, rather than continually, as we too often do, introducing the questions of crisis and deficits. And there, the Council is excellent.

I noted that in Aix-en-Provence, Angela Merkel and Nicolas Sarkozy even announced in advance a reform of the treaties, without having discussed anything with Mr. Van Rompuy, in order to sanction all states that fail to respect budgetary discipline tomorrow.

This is where we stand today with regard to the Council, while Parliament is asking for one thing, and in particular from the Commission, to try to advance the European project—and this is happening through the budget.
Then you have noted a number of points on which we entirely agree.
There is a need for greater stability.
Of course, we must find together a way to have a much more flexible budget, as this concerns essential needs and Europe's ability to respond promptly.
It also involves, of course, setting priorities and organizing the efficiency of the European budget.
We will all agree on this, absolutely, it is a necessity.
But where we must be clear with one another is that we need a schedule, we must jointly establish objectives, Commissioner. It cannot remain merely words, mere commitments, mere

Texts that are furthermore very interesting and that I share almost 100%. But now, we must move into the concrete, particularly regarding an essential issue around which we keep circling: own resources.
How do we secure our own resources for tomorrow for Europe—not for Europe alone, but for all European citizens—and together regain the path to growth, a path even stronger for Europe?
It is up to you, Sir.
Mr. President, I would like to focus my remarks on the preparation for the upcoming G20 summits.

Everything has already been said by the previous speakers regarding European economic governance, on the need to strengthen European economic governance, to reinforce the Stability and Growth Pact, and to involve the European Parliament—and of course also national parliaments—more effectively in the process.
I would like to briefly mention two challenges facing our nations and the members of the G20, which, in my view, must be addressed within the framework of the upcoming G20 meetings.
The challenge of the currency war is that of the volatility of commodity prices.
I believe that on these two issues, which represent genuine threats to global growth and cause very significant imbalances on our planet, the Union must

European Union manages to organize itself, to adopt common positions, and to equip itself to speak with one voice at the upcoming G20 summits, as well as those that follow, and more broadly, on the world stage. Thank you.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, I would first like to congratulate our colleague Diogo Feyo on the quality of the report and on the ambitious proposals it contains.
It also demonstrates that the European Parliament is fully asserting its role in a debate that is crucial for Europe's future, and we can only welcome this development.
Moreover, the Greek crisis served as a revealing indicator of the dysfunctions undermining the economic governance of the European Union.
I note, on this point, the proposal by Mr. Feio to establish a permanent mechanism for financial stability.
We must also address the problem at its root.
To this end, I believe the solution also lies in strengthening the Stability and Growth Pact, and in particular its sanctions.
This is essential if we are to achieve a lasting improvement in the fiscal situation of states, which is not always popular.
It is uncomfortable, but we have no choice.

Mr. President, I believe the report clearly highlights the challenges posed by the marine environment in terms of fishing, aquaculture, transportation, tourism, energy, mineral and biological resources, research, and the combined impact of climate change.
It is a considerable development potential that must be secured, protected, and sustainably enhanced, as the future of humanity depends on it.
The report also rightly notes that the European Union is the world's leading maritime power and that these outermost regions provide it with the largest exclusive economic zone in the world.
Given such a situation, it is therefore up to the European Union to implement an ambitious policy, a major maritime policy.
If it is not the world's leading maritime power doing it, someone else will do it in its place.
But it must be noted that the resolution does not go very far in this direction.
One cannot claim leadership in global politics with just 50 million euros.
Finally, although the outermost regions have a recognized strategic position, given their presence in all oceans, it would have

It would have been prudent to assign them a major role in a comprehensive ocean strategy, a proposal which, unfortunately, was not retained in the report, and for which I regret the omission. Thank you, Mr. President.
Madam, Sir, I would like to return to the anti-counterfeiting agreement currently being negotiated, particularly with the United States.
This agreement has also raised questions in the United States, particularly among officials at the U.S. Patent Office.
Moreover, seventy-five American law professors have recently sent a letter to President Obama.
He considers that ACTA, this mere executive agreement, would be contrary to the United States Constitution.
while the European Parliament is about to, as you know, adopt a resolution on the ACTA agreement, which continues to raise major concerns and is far from being universally supported
Among our fellow citizens, I believe it would be helpful to receive a very clear response from our American counterparts. On two points: Can ACTA be ratified by the United States?
Is ACTA perceived by them as a binding or a voluntary agreement?

Indeed, an anti-counterfeiting agreement lacking participation from China, India, and Brazil—countries already absent—and which would not be implemented by the United States would greatly lose credibility.
Thank you.

Mr. President, throughout this difficult process of adopting the alternative investment funds directive, I have consistently advocated for the principle of equal treatment between fund managers, regardless of whether they are based in the European Union or not.
This principle sounds like an obvious truth, but its self-evidence has encountered countless difficulties.
The result presented to us today is satisfactory in nature.
I thank the negotiators, and above all our rapporteur, Mr. Gasès, who, following this particularly painful delivery for him, certainly deserves a substantial maternity leave.
I would especially like to emphasize that the issue of treating third countries must still be viewed from two perspectives, whether one is inside or outside the European Union.
Thanks in particular to the European Parliament, all funds sold within the European Union will effectively be subject to comparable regulation.
One can therefore speak, more or less, of a love playing field.

It remains that, during the transitional period, non-EU entities will benefit from the national placement regime, while EU funds and managers will be subject to the directive, which is generally stricter.
The situation is different on markets located outside the European Union. There, European funds and their managers are subject to the directive, which is not the case for funds from third countries.
It would therefore be much more difficult to sell European products around the rest of the world, as they would be less flexible and more culturally specific.
We must be aware of this disadvantage, even though it was partly unavoidable.

Regarding depositaries, knowing that we will also make the exercise more demanding—particularly due to their new responsibilities—the increased costs should lead to substantial changes in their profession.

Mr. President, I am not saying this to demonstrate my opposition, but to emphasize that this new regulation is by no means trivial, and its implementation, which is necessary, will also entail difficulties that we must be aware of.

Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, particularly at the beginning of the 21st century, patients need appropriate information in order to be fully involved in their care pathway, to know where to seek care, to decide on the most suitable treatment in agreement with healthcare professionals, and to strictly follow the prescribed treatment.
Today, patients demand independent, comparative, and personalized information tailored to their individual needs.
Unfortunately, the revision proposals for the directive submitted by the European Commission did not adequately address the needs expressed by European citizens.
These proposals opened the door to direct promotion by pharmaceutical companies, which we consider nonsensical in terms of addressing patients' actual needs.
This operation holds no interest for either citizens or member states.
On the contrary, it meant additional bureaucracy, increased expenses, and putting patients at risk.
For these reasons, we initially presented a drafted version of these texts, thereby requesting the Commission to revise its draft.

This approach, also adopted by my colleague Carl Schulte from the Greens group, has enabled our rapporteur, Mr. Fechner, whom I salute, to carry out a significant revision of the Commission's proposals.

Meanwhile, we had submitted approximately sixty amendments aimed at further protecting the health of our citizens and facilitating patients' access to independent, comparative information tailored to individual needs.

The adoption of these amendments in the Environment Committee enabled greater emphasis to be placed on patients' rights to information, rather than making the provision of information an option for the pharmaceutical industries.

Given the compromise amendments achieved, we have decided to withdraw our position of rejection of the texts, as the substance of the text has evolved in a positive direction over the past few months.
The draft report, as amended, has managed to counterbalance the European Commission's initial proposals.

However, we will continue to oppose the instrumentalization of healthcare professionals in distributing patients, brochures, and information provided by pharmaceutical companies without any oversight by health authorities.
Similarly, we will oppose the authorization of industrial campaigns, information campaigns, particularly regarding vaccination.
Information cannot freely come from pharmaceutical companies.

Experience shows that we must be extremely vigilant, and indeed, what has occurred with the mediator drugs demonstrates just how vigilant we must be and how essential it is to maintain that balance we have sought through this draft report.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, Madam Minister, unity and realism will be reflected, at least I hope so, in tomorrow’s vote on our resolution concerning the 16th Conference of the Parties on climate change.

I therefore hope that our commissioner in charge will be able to pursue the practical objectives we collectively need to achieve, as well as defend the non-negotiable conditions set by Europe that are associated with them.
In these negotiations, we must likewise not lose sight of the legitimate expectations of fairness held by our fellow citizens and our industries.
It is the world that must combat climate change, not Europe alone.
Thus, recognizing the importance of intellectual property that is insufficiently protected will be essential.

Green technologies must benefit as many people as possible, but we must not, for that reason, undervalue our current and future knowledge, nor compromise those very assets in this indispensable fight against climate change.

Similarly, requesting common calculation methods for emissions, along with verification and publication of data, so that all industries and entities are subject to the same standard, appears to be a perfectly legitimate request, not a negotiating tactic.
Finally, more controversial—and my colleague raised this point earlier—gaps in the current system of clean development mechanisms must also be addressed.
Dear colleagues, we are all fully aware, albeit regretfully, that the true negotiating power does not lie with the European Parliament.

But the determination of many European deputies, including myself, to work toward and contribute to a positive outcome of this conference must be encouraged, not overlooked, as we have felt in recent days.
If Europe wishes to be strong within international dialogues, it must be equally strong among all these legislators.
Madam President, the European Union often presents economic partnership agreements as an opportunity for African countries.
She therefore despair of not seeing these agreements signed as soon as possible?
But the question that concerns us is why these countries do not rush to sign agreements that are favorable to them.
Clearly, opinions differ regarding the quality of these agreements, and as long as these differences in assessment persist, ACP countries will remain reluctant to sign them.
Would it not be appropriate to go further in dialogue and understanding by listening to the concerns and expectations of the ACP countries?
These ones wish to discuss development strategies, whereas Europe often responds merely with market liberalization.
It is this listening that the ACP-EU Parliamentary Assembly imposes upon itself.
I believe that other European bodies should adopt the same approach if we are to establish a genuine strategic partnership between Africa and the European Union.
Mr. President, Christians are undoubtedly one of the most persecuted religious groups in the world.

In the Middle East, they are perceived by certain extremist groups as, and I quote, legitimate targets, with the aim being their outright departure from this region—this region that gave birth to Christianity and in which their historical presence is essential to a certain balance.
The events at the cathedral in Baghdad are merely the culmination of a situation we have passively observed for far too long.
The European Union, as a global actor and trading partner of many countries in this region, must strongly express its indignation at the situation.
Discrimination of any kind cannot be tolerated.
Democracy will only prevail in this region when the most fundamental human rights, such as religious freedom, are respected.
My colleagues, we are all deeply concerned by the serious crisis currently prevailing in Côte d'Ivoire.

It is clear that the decision by this country's Constitutional Council to challenge, in violation of the electoral code, the election results officially proclaimed by the Independent Electoral Commission, has been unanimously condemned by the international community.

In the European Union, the United Nations, the African Union, the Parity Parliamentary Assembly of the European Union-ACP, the OAC, and soon undoubtedly the European Parliament, demand respect for the official proclamation of the.

election results and the installation of the new President of the Republic, as recognized by the Independent Electoral Commission. Independent Electoral Commission, which, I remind you, is an institution validated by the United Nations.
All political groups in the European Parliament expressed, in a joint statement, their agreement on these essential points.

They also called for preventing any escalation of tension within the country that could lead to civil war, which would be a human, social, and economic disaster for Côte d'Ivoire—something that must obviously be avoided at all costs.

Madam President, I would like in turn to thank our four rapporteurs, who have carried out outstanding work and have significantly enhanced the European Parliament’s contribution to the text initially proposed by the Commission.
The citizen initiative we are about to adopt with enthusiasm under the PPE is a major advancement of the Lisbon Treaty.
Let us not be deceived: this instrument can truly transform the political landscape of Europe, provided it is used with both responsibility and courage. I repeat: responsibility and courage.
It can help bring European citizens directly into the European political debate.

It can help foster a European public opinion, meaning men and women who are citizens of Europe coming together to debate, take collective positions, and unite around issues that are genuinely European.
But let us not disappoint our fellow citizens.
We urge our member states to take swift implementation measures where necessary. Let us not disappoint our fellow citizens; this is essential for strengthening European democracy.
Mr. President, I would like, in turn and like several of my colleagues, to address the issue of the persecution of Christians throughout the world, particularly Christians of the East.
There is no need to recall the horrible acts recently committed.
I would like to emphasize once again that defending Christians in the East, as elsewhere in the world, advocating for religious freedom, the diversity of
To believe in it is to advocate for freedom, democracy, and human rights—values at the heart of the European project, which are, of course, universally recognized here as universal values.
It is truly time now, beyond outrage, emotion, and solemn condemnations—of course—truly time now to move from words to action.
I urgently call upon the authorities of the European Union, the High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy, as well as all our leaders, the ministers of foreign affairs.
Undertake specific measures to implement a precise, concrete action plan to make respecting religious diversity and combating all forms of religious persecution a priority. I thank you.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, High Representative, we all agree that the situation in the Sahel region has significantly deteriorated.
And allowing a zone of lawlessness to take root at the port of Europe is a danger we must firmly and immediately confront.
Extremist groups are multiplying and threatening the lives of civilian populations.
On my part, I deplore the loss of human life and the hostage takings, and I am concerned about European nationals present on the ground.
To date, Morocco had served as a barrier against all kinds of trafficking originating from the Sahel.
Nowadays, drug, arms, and human traffickers circumvent Morocco by passing through Mauritania to the Canary Islands, en route to the European Union.
It seems to me that it is the responsibility of the European Union to act in order to assist these states in putting an end to these threats.
Europe's security is played out on our territory, but also in its immediate neighborhood.
It is time for us to act, and I call on the Commission and the Council to implement an action plan to help restore security in the Sahel.
It has already been stated, but I associate myself with the expressions of sympathy and support that have already been extended to the peoples of Tunisia and Egypt.
It represents a symbol of hope for all defenders of freedom.
A thought also for the victims.

I would now like to say that the European Union, which, as has been noted, has been criticized for its lack of response, must stand firmly alongside the peoples of Tunisia and Egypt to help them successfully open their countries to reform and establish democracy.

The European Union has decided to support Tunisian leaders in embarking on a peaceful transition, preparing for the upcoming elections to achieve freedom, and enabling the Tunisian people to attain prosperity, development, and social peace, along with an economy that will provide employment opportunities for youth.

I would conclude by saying that the Union for the Mediterranean faces a significant challenge, as the Jasmine Revolution has triggered a wave across all the countries of the Maghreb, the Middle East, and the Near East.
She must implement a robust strategy toward these countries.

But beware, in the face of this legitimate democratic movement, Europe must find a balance between non-interference in internal affairs, support for the legitimate aspirations of peoples, and stability.
Commissioner, my colleagues have reminded him as soon as he regained consciousness.

At a time when Europe is questioning a common strategy for its raw material supply, and particularly for rare earths, we cannot overlook the immense potential represented by the intelligent valorization of our waste.

Strengthening European standards to improve the functioning of collection chains, address issues of leakage or illegal export of used oils, and increase our collection targets also requires full commitment to this legislation.
Let us not forget the main actor in the success or failure of this regulation: the consumer.
Citizen accountability is crucial if we are to establish effective legislation,
And to this end, my dear Caroline, the eco-contribution has proven to be one of the most relevant tools, among other systems.

Electrical and electronic equipment producers must, of course, become more involved in collection and recycling systems, but citizens cannot be exempted from responsibility for the waste they generate.
Our citizens must be actors, not spectators, of European environmental policy.
Madam President, Mr President, Commissioner, I would like to echo the remark just made by Mrs McGuinness regarding the essential link between food issues and agricultural matters.
The issue that concerns us is the fluctuation of food prices and the consequences it may have on various populations around the world, as well as in Europe.
I would first like to try to share an observation.

The trend toward rising agricultural prices and agricultural commodity prices is, in my view, a long-term trend that will persist for three fundamental reasons: global demographic development and increasing demand

the world population. Second, the fact that we have entered, along with global warming, a period of climatic anomalies that pose problems, whether droughts or floods. The third point, because the world is also developing.
With emerging new countries, consumption levels are increasing.
Pressure on agricultural markets and agricultural raw materials is a major trend.
It is exacerbated by price volatility, and price volatility is a consequence of the financialization of the global economy.
It is precisely on this issue that we must also focus if we are to address both the agricultural and the food-related questions.

To achieve this, what the Commissioner has said is significant. There are two key elements: yes, there must be transparency regarding stockpiles, but there must also be a public policy on stockpiles both in Europe and global stock management, particularly at the level of the United Nations.

Second point: financial markets and financial speculation must be regulated, particularly by ensuring that speculative volumes no longer have any connection whatsoever with the physical quantities traded on these markets.

We cannot accept that, on the issue of food, speculators engage in speculation without regard for the consequences, since we are dealing with the world's and humanity's food supply. I conclude by saying
This leads me to two major consequences: we need a strong European policy, and we strategically reaffirm Europe's commitment to being a global player in food and agriculture.
Second, we need to revise our development policy so that, at the global and European levels, we once again invest in agriculture, as we will need all forms of agriculture to feed the world.
Madam President, Commissioner, as in 2008, agricultural commodity prices are rising on global markets?
We are only at three percent of the level from 2008.
the decline in production in certain traditional exporting regions due to climate change, the diversion of cereals toward biofuel production, and speculation
Financial institutions that amplify upward effects are responsible for this situation, as shown by reports from the IMF and French authorities, as well as by the Commission.
The consequences of this new food crisis are severely affecting the 1.2 billion people worldwide who live below one dollar a day.
Since October, forty million additional people have joined those already living below the poverty line.
Two days before the G20 meeting, what is Europe waiting for to take similar measures to those passed in the United States?
Since January 13, 2011, the U.S. administration has required the limitation and transparency of positions taken by financial funds in agricultural commodity markets.
This advancement marks the first step toward countering the appetites of speculators.
The impact is also dramatic for European farmers.
They can no longer feed their animals.
Hundreds of farms are bankrupt.
Five years ago, wheat was worth 100 euros per tonne. Today, it is trading at nearly 300 euros.
The situation has therefore completely changed.
The Commission demands that farmers adapt to market signals. Today, it would do well to follow its own advice.
Faced with this crisis, the European Union cannot afford thirty-two thousand fourteen to find solutions.
We must confront the crisis, and I propose three concrete actions.
First, the creation of grain stocks in cereal-producing countries to make feed available to livestock farmers at subsidized prices immediately.
Second, indexing livestock meat prices paid to farmers to the evolution of livestock feed costs.
Thirdly, for 2011, a mechanism transferring a portion of the subsidies intended for cereal support to livestock production. Thank you.
Mr. President, are we ready in Europe to face a pandemic?
The answer is yes, but there is a caveat.
The 2009–2010 H1N1 influenza episode showed us that each Member State reacted on its own behalf, without much coherence, nor even solidarity.

As the fictitious rapporteur on this file, I believe it was necessary to make these observations, but above all to go further, to be constructive and concrete for our citizens. Let us say it plainly: this is, in my view, what is most regrettable—public trust in public health messages has undoubtedly been somewhat eroded.

Ms. Rivasi's report, which will be voted on tomorrow, takes into account her fundamental priorities, among which are the systematic strengthening of cooperation among Member States, and improved coordination with the

European health institutions, a clarification of the roles of European health risk management structures, and an assessment of the vaccination and communication strategies implemented by the Member States.
It is therefore a good report. I congratulate the rapporteur. Naturally, it must of course not remain a dead letter.
It is the responsibility of our member states, which retain the majority of competences in this area. It is therefore a genuine responsibility that must be upheld toward our citizens.

Mr. President, I too wish to express my profound emotion following the cowardly assassination last March of Mr. Babar, the Pakistani minister responsible for religious minorities, particularly Christians.
This terrorist murder follows numerous other assassinations of courageous, tolerant, human rights-loving Pakistanis—human rights and women's rights defenders, I must say.

Once again, we urgently call on the Pakistani government to thoroughly investigate this cowardly assassination and to effectively, truly effectively, protect individuals threatened by fanatical religious extremists.
I think, of course, above all, but not exclusively, of Madame Chery Raymond.
I also wished, once again, forcefully, to call upon all competent institutions of the European Union to include in the negotiations aimed at
prepare future cooperation agreements, particularly the cooperation agreement between the European Union and Pakistan, including a clause on respect for religious freedom and human rights.
Mr. President, for the record, due to the crisis and the bursting of the real estate and credit bubble, the Irish State had to intervene to support the banks.
Ireland's public deficit, the cumulative budget balance of central, local, and particularly social security administrations, is estimated at 32 percent of gross domestic product in 2010.

If the Irish government, which finds it difficult to refinance itself on financial markets, wishes to obtain loans from the mechanism and the European Stability Mechanism, it will have to meet obligations regarding social and fiscal reforms, even though these areas fall under the principle of subsidiarity.
In our view, this is not contradictory.
As a physician, I would say that in the case of fever, it serves no purpose to prescribe antipyretics blindly without seeking to treat the underlying cause.
In the event of the allocation of these funds, it is up to that State to carry out a correction of its finances in order to eliminate its excessive deficit and rectify its prior errors.
This austerity plan should increase GDP by ten percent over the next four years.

Just as a private individual, a bank will not intervene in their personal life or management but will condition a personal loan on a guarantee of solvency, so too can the European Union lend to Ireland based on consolidated solvency.
without interfering in its private management, the bank will encourage the individual to negotiate, for example, a salary increase or to borrow elsewhere.

Thus, respecting subsidiarity, the European Union, based on an agreement established with the IMF, considers that this solvency requires reducing the statutory minimum wage and adjusting pensions, without exempting itself from fiscal measures.
It is up to Ireland to decide whether or not to implement these measures.

It is not a free door to European Union interference in areas of subsidiarity, which could, for example, dangerously impose a European minimum wage level, but rather a protection of the stability of the eurozone through a process of mutual assurance.

Of course, we should assist Ireland, as with other struggling countries, which also protects us; but not at any price, particularly not one that involves allowing a state unable to repay its debts in the long term, thereby undermining both its own citizens and those of the entire European Union.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, the eruption of the Icelandic volcano last April, as many of us have just said, provided a painful illustration of the fragmentation and inefficiency of European airspace.
Each of us here in this chamber has been directly affected.
Five million of our fellow citizens found themselves stranded, with no viable alternative means of transportation available.
And a year after this exceptional event, new European measures are still awaited.

A first crisis exercise, as you mentioned, Mr. Commissioner, related to volcanic ash is scheduled for the 13th and 14th of April next year, under the coordination of Eurocontrol from ICAO and dDIATA; however, it is essential that the ESA assume genuine leadership in coordinating safety measures at the European level.
It is certain that the information provided by the waves from London and Toulouse must be refined using more modern technological means.
These data, as noted last April, can however be contradictory and may lead each Member State to make decisions regarding the closure of its airspace less motivated by information than by emotion.
In this context, the role of the ESA is essential in providing a harmonized interpretation of these data, as well as ensuring the centralization and effective dissemination of information.
Beyond informing the VACs, the AISAA must establish a data collection mechanism by closely involving the Member States, airlines, and their pilots.
This centralization of information and coordination of all involved parties will prevent the chaos and inconsistencies we witnessed last year.
I therefore expect the Commission, in collaboration with ESA, to swiftly present concrete proposals fully aligned with the achievement of the Single European Sky.
Dear colleagues, this tax is important.
This is not a revolution. One cannot claim that everything was bad before and everything is perfect now.
Nevertheless, it does present a number of significant advances at this provisional stage of our work.

In my view, the main issues concern better regulation of distance sales, and in particular those conducted over the internet—and this is Chapter 3, as consumer rights in this area need to be strengthened.
E-commerce is highly beneficial: it broadens the consumer's choice, enables them to obtain the lowest prices, and also allows small and medium-sized enterprises and craftsmen to access new markets.

To make progress in this area, it was necessary to do so in a genuinely European manner, that is, by focusing on a few specific points and establishing a comprehensive harmonization of everyone's rights and obligations.
Very concretely, it was essential that an Italian customer purchasing from a Belgian or German website should know that, from now on, they would enjoy the same rights and the same protection.
This is what this directive will make possible.
This means that its withdrawal period will be extended to fourteen days throughout the Union.
This means he immediately pays the full amount he will owe, without fear of an unpleasant surprise later.
This means that, thanks to the double click, it will properly verify its command.
Of course, all of this will disrupt certain habits.
In many respects, there are subtle nuances in national law that each party considers fundamental, yet which are sometimes nowhere to be found in neighboring countries.
But living together in Europe means learning to slightly adjust one's habits in the service of the common and general interest.
Let us be clear: this text will still need to be improved, and we will have to do so in the coming weeks in collaboration with the Council and the Commission.
I have appreciated the openings that have just been made in this regard.
But we have already carried out substantial work, thanks to our rapporteur André Ashchoab, whom I warmly thank.
But the real outcome of this work is that the amendments will be voted on tomorrow by all political groups.
Consumer confidence in the European Union's internal market is very fragile.
This political unanimity was necessary to preserve it and to hope to strengthen it.
At point number seven, after the first paragraph, a first subparagraph shall ensure respect for human rights and fundamental freedoms, in particular the
Freedom of speech and assembly, and the right to peacefully demonstrate, as well as to hear and address the legitimate demands of demonstrators and ensure their safety.
Madam President, dear colleagues, Commissioner, the agreement reached between Europe and the United States is a step forward, but it is only one step that places Europe at a crossroads.
The Transport Committee and the Commission itself have emphasized this point.
Either we accept an agreement that disadvantages European businesses, or we suspend the agreement and risk losing the benefits gained since 2008.
Let me therefore draw a parallel with private and professional pilot licenses, a text that will be discussed before summer, as the pilots I belong to are facing a dilemma.
The European Aviation Safety Agency wishes to harmonize licenses across the European territory, and it will do so at the expense of the eighty-nine thousand general aviation pilots.
Directly affected, some holding only a single U.S. license, and at the expense of—this is crucial—businesses and jobs related to this activity.
I would like to draw your attention to this project, which lacks a security basis.
There are thirty-one times fewer accidents among holders of an American license.

The objectives of the baseline regulation text 216 allow for the recognition and equivalence of licences whose benefits do not justify delaying their recognition until hypothetical agreements postponed to 2014.
Thus, I propose an open sky agreement and even invite you to accompany me when I occasionally come to Parliament, personally flying to demonstrate that an American pilot's license is entirely safe.
Madam President, at this stage of our debate, I am inevitably going to repeat or emphasize certain points that have already been made.

But I also wanted to say that, like many of my colleagues, the challenges we are facing—challenges that the entire European Union is currently confronting—cannot be resolved by any single Member State alone.
No Member State should be left alone facing the challenges it encounters, which, once again, in my view, confront the entire European Union.
I am therefore calling precisely for the European Union's capacity to organize itself and to respond collectively and solidarity-based.

Mr. President, in its Europe 2020 strategy, the European Union has set itself the objective of making our economy greener and our transport systems more modern.

on the impact of climate change in ACP countries, the impact of the global crisis, or on media freedom and independence and food security.
