I had the honor of co-chairing this mission in Burundi for the presidential elections alongside my colleague Assa, Member of Parliament from Mali.
This election observation mission was a genuine success.

We were able to meet with all the relevant political stakeholders, and thanks to the dual origin of this delegation—both African and European—we embodied a legitimacy distinct from that of European or African missions.
I therefore invite the Bureau of the PP to renew this experience, which enables practical application of solidarity and the privileged relationships linking the European Union to the ACP countries.
No, I wanted to thank Mr. Darden for his report
Certainly, the crisis is economic and financial, but above all, for our fellow citizens, it is a social crisis.
Reducing by twenty million the number of people in precarious situations by 2020 requires strong measures involving all levels of public authority.

We must more than ever fulfill the commitments made by Europe in 2010, during the European Year for Combatting Poverty and Social Exclusion, and in establishing the European Platform against Poverty and Social Exclusion, in order to have a European framework for social and territorial cohesion.

We must ensure the sustainability and optimization of European funds, particularly the current program supporting the most vulnerable, strengthen social protection, and support local or national initiatives that develop services for the care of children, elderly individuals, and dependent persons.
Poverty is a scourge to be fought horizontally, and it is high time to act in order to enable the most disadvantaged to overcome their precarious situation.

As political leaders, at the European, national, and local levels, we must take the necessary measures to ensure, without discrimination, their access to housing, access to healthcare, access to justice, access to education, and access to a first job with a minimum income that may be indexed according to each Member State's poverty threshold.
Investing in the promotion of innovation and social experimentation, as well as in poverty reduction, will enable us to establish a sustainable, smart, and inclusive growth strategy.
The Europe we desire and currently need is a Europe based on solidarity.
Mr. President, I would first like to congratulate the rapporteur, Ms. Levine, on her excellent work.

As rapporteur for the opinion of the Development Committee, I am pleased that the Fisheries Committee has taken up the elements contained in our opinion, as it is crucial that the development dimension
shall be taken into account in measures aimed at combating illegal fishing globally, in the interest of policy coherence and the close links with development policy.
Indeed, combating illegal fishing requires comprehensive solutions and the full implementation by all states, including developing ones.
However, it is necessary that the technical and financial capacities of states are also taken into account in the actions to be undertaken.
Indeed, one cannot impose the same constraints without accompanying measures on developed or developing countries.
Small island states, for example, have very limited capacities.
They must therefore be supported in their efforts, aimed at combating illegal fishing.
For this reason, the Union will need to increase the financial envelope supporting the fishing sector when concluding agreements in this field with developing countries.

Moreover, it is crucial that measures aimed at combating illegal fishing be implemented, as this practice undermines the development of countries by reducing the financial resources these nations could otherwise derive from the economic activity of fishing.
Otherwise, these resources are necessary for developing countries to implement social, economic, and educational policies.

Finally, it is important that the European Union promote, in implementing these measures to combat illegal fishing in developing countries, the involvement of civil society and businesses alongside the recipient states.
To adopt a holistic approach, which is essential for curbing this scourge of illegal fishing. Thank you.
Mr. President, I would like to say that largely thanks to these outermost regions, the European Union is the world's leading maritime power.
This represents a major advantage for Europe, and therefore the development and implementation of a comprehensive policy on seas, oceans, and seabed is essential for the future.
But I fear that with 20 or 50 million euros, it will not be enough to meet this major challenge.

Combating illegal fishing is part of this broader policy, and it is urgent at the European level to find an appropriate solution to this issue, particularly within the framework of international cooperation.
I would also like to point out that the maritime zones of Guadeloupe, French Guiana, Martinique, Mayotte, and Réunion are regularly violated and plundered.
Since the report does not address this issue, I take this opportunity to draw the attention of my colleagues and the Commission to this particular matter.
Madam President, I would first like to greet the Polish Presidency, which has demonstrated dynamism and determination.
Next week, at the Council, our heads of state and government will have to make essential decisions for the future of the eurozone and for the future of the Union.
They will also have to seriously address the revival of growth.

That said, we clearly see that today, financial stability has become the top priority, and the pressures and tensions emanating from outside the euro area, as well as those within it, are undermining our credibility and eroding confidence.
to hope for the return of confidence, financial stability must be restored, and the alarming rise in national deficits and sovereign debts must be halted. therefore
Financial stability has truly become the top priority, an urgent obligation for everyone.

Our democracies have moreover all committed themselves to very courageous programs for cleaning up their public finances and reducing their debts; yet financial stability is also a prerequisite for any recovery in growth.
But whoever speaks of solidarity also speaks of financial stability, budgetary discipline, because, of course, loss must also imply solidarity.
We believe it is important to ensure this dual movement toward financial stability, budgetary discipline, and solidarity.
One final word about democracy.
Beware of disempowering our democratic institutions.

Rather than resorting to a judicial mechanism, I would say that in cases of budgetary deviations, we should instead consider mechanisms of oversight and sanctions carried out by democratic institutions.

To be perfectly frank, I am not the spokesperson for the French Minister of Foreign Affairs, and quite frankly, even though I belong to the same political party as him, I am not constantly, I would say, in a position to relay the statements of the French Minister of Foreign Affairs.
To be perfectly frank once again, I am not aware of any recent statements by Alain Jué to that effect.
I simply believe that France's current position is to continually assert and reassert that the euro is truly the heart of our European construction, that safeguarding the euro is that

the safeguarding of this common project and the safeguarding of the European Union, and in this effort to preserve the euro, we must of course look to the future, think about protecting younger generations, and preserving peace on our continent
Mr. President, dear colleagues, tomorrow we will vote for the third time on the new instrument for development cooperation
development that has led to accompanying measures in the banana sector for ACP banana-exporting countries following the reduction of their tariff preference, the ACP Banana Agreement.
This aid program has a budget of 190 million euros and targets ten ACP countries until 2013.
It is of crucial importance to our African and Caribbean partners, as the banana sector holds significant economic and social weight in these countries.
Although none of the Parliament's claims regarding the application of delegated acts have been incorporated into this instrument, I regret this strongly.
We have nevertheless managed to achieve progress.
Indeed, the text sets out clear objectives and precise criteria for allocating resources to the Commission.
The Commission also issued a statement specifying the methodology employed.

Moreover, we have a statement from the Council on the next multiannual financial framework, specifying that it will duly take into account the delegated acts in the external aid instruments after 2013.
Finally, in its actual resolution, the Parliament clarifies—and this point seems crucial to me—that this is in no way a precedent.

I would like to tell you that I have just returned from Togo, where the 22nd session of the ACP-EU Joint Assembly took place, and I believe I can confidently say that our ACP partners are counting on us, given the urgency of the situation.
The funds should have been released two years ago.
We must be aware of the regrettable consequences for our ACP partners if the BAM initiative were rejected.
The situation of certain farmers is dramatic. We can no longer wait and must act accordingly.
I therefore rely on you to support this text. Thank you.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, our coordinator Tis Beman is ultimately unable to be present this evening for personal reasons.
However, I know that he fully shares my analysis on this matter.

The negotiations on this legislative package were long and tedious, and although the outcome regarding the human rights instruments and ICI+ is satisfactory, we are still very far from achieving our goals concerning the ordinary instrument.

Thus, the compromise reached on this text would tomorrow undermine the mandate given to our negotiation team, which was to place the Parliament on equal footing with the Council on all programming decisions concerning the selection of scientific priorities and financial allocations.
Because this concerns powers belonging to our Assembly and the very spirit of the Lisbon Treaty.
The choice of programming priorities is a highly political decision in which our Assembly must have its say.
By categorically refusing to recognize any form of parliamentary oversight and resorting to shameful tactics, the Council once again demonstrates a condemnable ideological retreat.
However, Member States must understand that the European Union's external action is now a shared competence between our institutions.
That is why our Parliament must remain sovereign and reject the proposal under the guise of banana.
It is the only way we have of showing the Council and the Commission that the Parliament desires greater transparency in community decisions.
If we lose this battle, absurdly, we would have to wait for a new treaty to finally obtain the powers already provided for by the Lisbon Treaty.
Mr. President, at the risk of offending those among you who are most federalist, I believe that a defense policy is eminently a national policy.
It meets the specific strategic, diplomatic, and security needs of each nation.
The sharing of capabilities is nothing other than a severing of sovereignty. The military equipment single market is a vast joke when certain member states consider that
Defense is secured in Washington, with priority given to American equipment. Europe is unable, in this field as in so many others, to enforce community preference.

With regard to the shared security challenges facing the member states of the Union, nothing exists that cannot be resolved through intergovernmental cooperation, independent of any involvement from Brussels.

I regret this, but entrusting these areas to Mr. Ashton would amount to acknowledging that Moody's and Standard & Poor's have legitimacy in judging our economic policies, and accepting that the Merkozy duo could impose a purely German Europe upon twenty-seven peoples—a mental aberration.

For thirty years and even longer, since the disappearance of the Soviet threat, defense budgets have been the first to be sacrificed on the altar of fiscal austerity, to the point of rendering most of our military tools ineffective.

deficient or dilapidated ones, and those abandoned for purely financial reasons, each expecting from the neighbor or the supposed American big brother the efforts that they themselves are no longer willing to make.
But you are mistaken in your perception.

Only by returning to independent and resolute national policies will we individually be able to acquire the necessary means, not only for our respective security and national interests, but also for cooperating these means to address common challenges.
A combination of weakness and deficiency has never made a strength.

Mr. President, I wished to express the French delegation's support for the European Treaty on Stability, Coordination, and Economic Governance, and its backing of the main conclusions reached by the Council.
Let us not be deceived by this.
This treaty represents a significant step towards greater economic and budgetary integration, aimed at enhancing financial stability.
Let us examine it here.
Sustainable revival of growth and employment is impossible without the consolidation of our public finances.
Of course, growth cannot be decreed; it is not a treaty alone that can revive growth.
But a treaty, that is, a set of rules and mutual commitments, can provide trust and accountability.

As the President of the Socialist Group felt it appropriate to introduce political maneuvering into this chamber, and by referring to the forthcoming French presidential campaign and mentioning the Socialist candidate in that French presidential election, I wished to respond.
we consider ourselves entirely irresponsible for the statements made by the French Socialist candidate who declared his intention to renegotiate the treaty concluded by twenty-five
Take negotiating what and with whom.
These words are entirely irresponsible.
They pay little attention to the French State's statement regarding respect for the French State's commitments.

Madame Ashton, Madagascar, one of the poorest countries in the world, is undergoing a political crisis whose resolution will depend on the implementation of the roadmap leading up to the presidential and legislative elections.
The growth of this country is negative.
Under normal circumstances, our contribution amounts to about fifty percent of this country's budget.
The European Union has rightly suspended its budgetary support for the ninth and tenth FED, but not for the MDGs.
Meanwhile, the population suffers, and businesses established in Madagascar are sometimes compelled to contribute, against their will.

While supporting your position, we wish for you to exert your full influence with the STEEC so that the legal and judicial uncertainty surrounding the return of a former president is resolved as soon as possible, as it constitutes one of the most sensitive aspects of implementing this roadmap.

I would appreciate your opinion on this matter, and I would also like to inform you that the European Union-Madagascar Friendship Group, which we have just established in the European Parliament, is available to work with you and the Commission in the interest of the Malagasy people and in defense of the values of our Union.

Mr. President, dear colleague, I would like to commend Mr. Vitourgaise, the rapporteur, for the quality of his work, for the lengthy negotiation process he had to undertake, and for the patience he has shown.

We have finally reached the vote on this report, which is extremely important, as it enables us to convey to this House, before the Commission and the Council, the difficulties and concerns of our fellow citizens.
Here is further evidence, if any were needed, of the effectiveness of the right of petition in Europe.
Petitions concerning the environment are predominant, and waste represents a significant concern for our fellow citizens.
This topic must address a societal issue.
We are spending recklessly.
We are only now beginning to realize that what we do not consume, we discard.
This awareness must absolutely be strengthened among citizens, that is clear.
But this report also serves as a reminder for the member states.
It is nonetheless concerning to see that states appear to be losing control of the situation.
Only six member states have transposed the Waste Framework Directive on time.
The number of complaints received on this matter sometimes reveals a lack of transparency in the selection of landfill locations.
Finally, and this is increasingly worrying, organized crime seems to be doing as it pleases.
A market worth 75 billion euros inevitably attracts greed.
States must send a strong signal.
Sanctions against companies that leak public contracts for personal gain must be increased.
This report serves as a warning signal to States, but the European Commission must also fulfill its role by ensuring the proper implementation of waste directives.
This file must be made visible, but also credible.
If no one plays by the rules, we will continue to waste time and money on verification and counter-verification procedures that may last for years.
Let us heed the petitioners' message and put an end to this wastefulness sooner.
Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Rapporteur, I would first like to congratulate Mr. Nicholson for the courage he has shown in facing the Council and the Commission.
Yet despite this, I fear that what we have achieved will not allow for a balance between producers and industrialists.
This agreement reminds me of that well-known illusion, the story children tell about a paradise where vegetarian lions graze beside lambs.
I don't believe it for a second. Since 2003, Mrs. Fischerbo has done everything possible to ideologically undermine the quality of the quataliers.
In October 2009, the Court of Auditors, despite everything, acknowledged the effectiveness of its litigation quotas.

Today, we are in a situation that will become even more dramatic, and we can see what is currently happening in Denmark, where the most solid farms, despite increased production, are all facing bankruptcy due to a 40% decline in land prices.
Do we want this for the European level?
I say no; we need control over production, with the involvement of public authorities. Thank you.
Madam Commissioner, the data protection standards of third countries still differ significantly from European standards.
It is nevertheless our duty to ensure that the minimum European standards of protection we uphold are respected and guaranteed to European citizens.
It is essential that personal data be processed lawfully and collected for specified and legitimate purposes.
European citizens must be guaranteed the right to access and rectify their personal data, as well as the right to bring a judicial remedy in the event of a violation of their rights.
Action must be taken to allow European and international standards to coexist and to resolve a potentially harmful legal conflict for European citizens.
European legislation alone is not sufficient.
It must be intelligently articulated and coexist with international standards, as we risk undermining our duties and commitments to European citizens.
This situation echoes other debates taking place within this assembly.
It reminds us of the relevance of initiatives such as the establishment of a European programme for monitoring terrorist financing.

Such a system would provide us with a tool that has proven its effectiveness in the United States, while fully respecting domestic law and thus ensuring the protection of personal data of European citizens.
European institutions must continue their commitment in this regard and fully uphold their protection standards. Thank you.
Mr. President, First of all, thank you to the rapporteurs for their work.

In response to the crisis, the European Union has spent more than a year strengthening its economic governance to prevent macroeconomic imbalances and excessive budgetary deficits threatening the European economy; however, this has not prevented either social exclusion or the rise in unemployment.

It is no longer a matter of time—urgency now demands that the objectives of the Europe 2020 strategy become an absolute priority for the European Commission and for the Member States, in the perspective of growth.

Each State will have to implement a national employment plan, meaning that emphasis should be placed on numerous issues such as, among others, training, career reorientation, microcredit, apprenticeships, paid internships, etc.

Or, and the commissioner himself just recalled it: we have twenty-three million unemployed people in Europe, but also twenty-three million small and medium-sized enterprises, and twenty-three billion euros in European funds dedicated to employment that remain unused.
We could almost make it a twenty-three twenty-three strategy.
Not to mention the 60 billion euros in unallocated cohesion funds.
In Denmark and the Netherlands, social initiatives have been implemented and proven effective based on the flexisécurité model.
Let us strive for it.

This also means that our overall social policy must be better adapted, with more favorable social incentives than those linked to unemployment, and active support for all socially responsible enterprises.
We had poverty reduction targets to achieve by 2020. Yet we already know they will not be met without strong and appropriate measures taken by the member states.

Our parliamentary role must therefore be fully exercised, and it is our responsibility to demand that the same energy devoted to implementing strong economic governance also be directed toward ambitious, effective, and binding social governance measures.
Madam President, undoubtedly, the cause of women owes much to Europe.
The principle of gender equality between men and women was already enshrined in the Treaty of Rome and was supposed to have been reaffirmed subsequently in numerous treaties.
Gender equality is truly a fundamental value of our European project.
As a result, Europe has always pursued gender equality and the advancement of women with great determination.
Little by little, progress was made.
Numerous legislative instruments have been adopted, and guidelines have been established.
Here, within our Parliament itself, for many years now, there has been an extremely active and vigilant Commission on Women's Rights and Gender Equality.
An European institute for gender equality has been established.
Programs are put in place, and financial resources are approved year after year.
More recently, on March 2, we had the opportunity to celebrate the second edition of the European Day for Equal Treatment, focusing on pay equality.
Undoubtedly, therefore, the legislative framework exists, and European will has been concretely manifested on multiple occasions.
To be frank, it must be acknowledged that the struggle remains ever relevant—the struggle for gender equality, and in particular, for equal pay.
On average, across all countries of the European Union, there is always a wage gap of the order of
For equal work, women always earn seventeen percent less than men.
This is of course entirely unacceptable.
The Women's Committee of the Parliament is currently working on a draft resolution calling on the European Commission to review the 2006 directive on equality of
treatment, which is one of the most pressing issues and at the very heart of the concerns and demands of European women in all our societies.

On another matter—namely, the establishment of quotas, both within the decision-making bodies of major corporations and within political life—I come from a country, France, which adopted such measures long ago
Ten years ago, binding rules on quotas were introduced in political life, and more recently, about one year ago, a law was enacted establishing quotas in the boards of directors of large companies.
France is not the only country, of course, to have adopted such legislation.
What is the assessment, after several years of application in one case, a few months in the other, to acknowledge that things have shifted, that progress has been made?
Thus, a necessary evil.
Quotas do make a difference.

Madam Commissioner, you have recently taken an important step toward establishing quotas at the European level in the decision-making bodies of major companies, by launching this major public consultation aimed at gathering opinions on the actions to be taken, and on any potentially legislative measures to be implemented.
On this matter, you have my full and complete support. Thank you.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, on the occasion of this resolution, allow me to address two issues: first, the challenge of water management worldwide, and in
Europe, and then a question to the European Commission so that actions can be taken to address the number of our concerns related to the common agricultural policy.
The issue, as we know, is that water is the major concern of the twenty-first century.
It is because we will have answers that we will have peace in the world and in Europe.
We are aware of the challenges, we are aware of the stakes in terms of management, the modern stakes in terms of food security.
And on this occasion, as our colleague Rivasi reminded us, too many purely commercial concerns lie at the heart of the discussion that has unfolded in recent days in Marseille.
A question: Why is there no coherence between water management and water-related resources and the Common Agricultural Policy?
Nothing in the reform of the CAP is specified regarding this management, as our colleague Sarbou mentioned a moment ago.
So give us answers.
Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, I would first like to thank the rapporteur and our team for the work accomplished.
This report now fully lives up to its name, namely, removing obstacles to citizenship.
Europe is a magnificent project, but it is all too frequently observed that it has not yet been fully defined.
Take, for example, the economic crisis.
It revealed our weaknesses and enabled us to strengthen more solidly the institutions regulating our single market.
For the European citizen, it is the same.
The Petitions Committee, a true office for complaints, records the grievances of our fellow citizens every day.
In three days, the European Citizens' Initiative will complete this framework.
Despite all our policies calling for mobility, integration, European values, and exchanges, there remain far too many obstacles to the exercise of citizenship.
And a citizen who does not feel European is a citizen who will not vote. It is a citizen who will not perceive the value that Europe represents in their daily life.
Along with my colleagues from the PPE, I have put forward a great many proposals, the greatest number of them, and I wish to thank them for this.
It is a sense of European belonging and identity that we must recreate and sustain through these measures.
Our Europe must be a Europe that protects.
Commissioner Viviane Reding has put forward twenty-five very concrete proposals in favor of citizens.
They are essential for restoring trust between the citizen and Europe.
But the proposals contained in this report reflect other expectations of our fellow citizens: online shopping, moving house, children's rights, employment, the
citizen participation, healthcare abroad, twinning initiatives, minority rights, etc. We still find it too difficult to fully take advantage of the capabilities offered by Europe.
Rights are exercised, claims are heard.
We are the ones who carry this forward here.
The European Year of Citizenship in 2013 must demonstrate to citizens that, with free movement, there is also free access to rights throughout Europe.
I believe that taxation is an interesting tool for shaping behavior.

I believe energy consumption should align with an indicative price signal for the consumer, and above all, I think that to avoid any distortion in the internal market, we must strive to bring our tax practices closer together.
The principle of proportionality among the various levels of taxation on energy products therefore appears to me to be a necessity.
In the case under consideration, for example, this would make it possible to put an end to the current diesel tourism, which is not an acceptable situation.
I therefore share the Commission's proposal for revising the directive's philosophy.

Nevertheless, if we are to achieve our objectives regarding CO2 emission reductions or energy efficiency, our collective decisions must on the other hand not penalize citizens or sectors particularly exposed to

price volatility on global markets, and I am thinking, for example, of the agricultural sector—the proposed directive will undoubtedly have a differentiated impact depending on each Member State's level of taxation, but still an impact on diesel prices.
The question is, who, ultimately, will have to bear this price increase?
Are there truly practical, realistic, and consumer-accessible alternatives?
As fictitious rapporteur in the Environment Committee, I support an ambitious, but above all sustainable, position for all.

I therefore advocate for greater progressivity and for the implementation of the proposal and its principle of proportionality to take effect in 2030, provided that a new impact assessment is conducted, updating the one upon which the Commission's proposal is based, which was carried out five years ago.

Earlier here, we had a debate on the wave of dismissals across Europe resulting from the economic crisis, particularly at Harcèlor, a multinational company present in nine European member states, which is restructuring its production facilities across Europe in response to declining steel demand.
Following the shutdown of two blast furnaces in Belgium and Luxembourg, and the temporary suspension of one in France, it is estimated that nearly six direct jobs were threatened in 2012.

Beyond unacceptable practices such as stoppengo, which ought to be better regulated, there is the broader issue of European steelmaking and adaptation to change, both of which demand extremely urgent responses.

Six months ago, the Commission informed us of the need to conduct, against the backdrop of the economic crisis, a new consultation exercise for responsible restructuring management in collaboration with social partners and public authorities.
My question is simple: six months later, where are we?

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues, I would first like to refute the unfounded allegations concerning the circulation in France of between 500 and one million counterfeit biometric passports allegedly circulating in our country.
France takes the risks of identity fraud very seriously.
She does not take offenses in this matter lightly.
At the initiative of its government, identity usurpation is a criminal offense punishable by one year in prison and a fine of 15 euros.
The conditions for issuing identity documents have been simplified, enabling prefectures to allocate additional resources to combat this fraud.
Thus, in each prefecture, a fraud liaison officer is designated.
Furthermore, the provisions regarding the security and issuance of identity documents have been strengthened.
For example, civil status data provided may be verified, to the greatest extent possible, between administrative services electronically, in order to minimize the risk of falsification.
Certain birth certificates are also issued on secure paper.
The same rigor applies to proof of address documents.
The Ministry of the Interior has committed to enhancing the security of proof of address documents by incorporating a barcode.

The standard processing of a title application includes authorized access to relevant files, comparison of all documents submitted in previous title applications, and, if necessary, authentication of supporting documents.

The security of title issuance is further ensured by the dual appearance of the applicant—once at the time of application submission, and again at the time of title collection—along with the capture of two fingerprint impressions.
And without mentioning the enhanced checks for renewal of lost or stolen documents, for comparative purposes with data retained by prefectures. I conclude.

Furthermore, France alerts international cooperation bodies, and in particular Interpol. I conclude, Mr. President; I am nearly finished with the passports issued, lost, or stolen, so that they cannot be used for fraudulent purposes.
Dear colleagues, France, like the network of the European Union, is committed.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, first of all, a fact: our fellow citizens increasingly wish to find and consume foods produced locally and in season, for reasons of health and quality in particular.
We must find ways to enable farmers to respond effectively and promptly to this growing demand.

We must support the establishment of local infrastructure for producers, such as maintaining local slaughterhouses, which, while ensuring strict sanitary oversight, enable the development of local short supply chains and strengthen direct sales.
Support for local agriculture and direct sales should help producers market their products and make them known to consumers.
Strengthening these direct relationships will enable farmers to retain a larger share of the added value generated on their farms.

Direct sales within farms should be encouraged and facilitated, and collective initiatives by farmers wishing to establish shared sales points should be supported, enabling local consumers to access a broader range of products.

The European Commission must facilitate the emergence, sustainability, and development of direct delivery systems to consumers, such as the amAP in France, which enable nearly three farmers to supply sixty households.
Finally, Member States must be able to add additional criteria, in particular concerning farm size, production volume, or the traditional nature of the product, in order to define local agriculture.
Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, I welcome the fact that this corrective budget concerning the funding of Italy was adopted by a large majority in the Committee on Budgets on March 29th.
By funding this additional contribution, we avoid discarding a strategic fundamental research project in which the European Union plays a leading role, and we fulfill our international commitments.
We recall that none of the seven partners in the Eutome community has challenged its overall contribution to the project.
Japan, despite the Fukushima tragedy, has decided to strictly honor its budgetary commitments to ITER.
Without this agreement, hundreds of researchers would have had to halt their work, and the ITER project would have suffered detrimental delays.
Today, a thousand people work for ITER full-time.
From 2015 until 2017 and 2018, the construction phase of the buildings and assembly of the machine could involve up to forty-five people at this site.
To conclude, Mr. President, ITER is a source of pride for Europe and a symbol of our excellence.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. rapporteur, thank you for the work we have accomplished together.
The European Union needs a manifesto, a shared political vision on the major challenges related to the environment by 2020 and beyond.
This is precisely the value of launching a seventh environmental action programme by synergizing existing financial frameworks and mobilizing new sources of funding.
This program must ensure coherence and coordination between the European Union's environmental strategies and sectoral policies, whether agriculture,
fishing, research and innovation, health, transport, regional development, territorial planning, and urban policies, of course.
Despite the economic crisis afflicting the European Union, we must not succumb to a short-term perspective and abandon all ambition in the environmental and climate domains.
Inaction would be fatal.
The stakes are too high, particularly with regard to the efficient use of our resources and the valuation of natural capital.
We must therefore firmly invest in the sustainable economy and eco-innovation, sources of growth, competitiveness, and employment.
It is our responsibility to promote eco-design, recycling, and environmental product labeling in order to prevent waste and misguided consumption choices.
We cannot overlook this essential environmental pedagogy for European citizens.
The environmental action program is a tool for political planning, but it is also a communication instrument.
It must provide the necessary clarity and predictability for national and local authorities, citizens, as well as entrepreneurs and investors, regarding the European Union's environmental choices.
European regulation must not become a punishment or a straitjacket.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, in the face of the profound transformations to which our world is currently subject, the Union must reform its system of generalized preferences.
Like any compromise, this one is no exception to the rule.
While satisfactory from a strictly commercial standpoint, it falls far short of being perfect. We must keep in mind that this regulation is intended for developing countries.
By developing its new paradigm of differentiation, the Commission states that it aims to refocus its action in favor of the poorest countries.
This does not appear entirely tenable at first glance.
To this end, the Commission has nevertheless developed a strictly accounting vision based solely on the criterion of GDP per capita.
One can clearly see the limitation of this approach.
What about many middle-income developing countries whose economies are poorly diversified and vulnerable to climate shocks?
The compromise reached with the Council does not address this issue, which is why I urge my colleagues to vote in favor of the three amendments to be submitted on this text.
Mr. Commissioner, the issue of the availability of public consultations in various languages was unfortunately not new to you.

This is a crucial issue in my view, which is why I have not only submitted several written questions to the European Commission since 2008, but have also personally addressed written questions to Mr. Barroso.

Indeed, the Commission must comply with the principle of equal access to information for all European citizens, as reminded by the Court of First Instance of the European Communities in its judgment of November 20, 2008.
I was entirely unsatisfied with the European Commission's response, according to which public consultations do not claim to provide a fully representative picture of all opinions.
According to this response, the Commission takes into account the fact that certain positions and viewpoints may be overrepresented at the expense of others.
That is not a response.
I am questioning the criteria that would allow us to measure the overrepresentation of the European population.
If written consultations are not representative due to the sole availability in English, for example, they fail to achieve their objective of consulting European citizens.
Thus, the result provides a misleading indication and should therefore not be used by the European Commission.
If we wish to maintain the consultations as a cornerstone of the European Commission's legislative proposals, we must ensure they are democratic, transparent, and fair for all citizens.
Thus, if the European Commission undertakes written circumcisions, it must assume its responsibilities and provide the necessary resources for multilingualism and the translation of questionnaires.
To conclude, I would like to add that multilingualism is not the only obstacle.

It would also be appropriate to include in the analysis the excessively short timeframes for submissions and contributions, as well as the limited efforts made by the Commission to communicate about these consultations, which remain unfortunately accessible mainly to insiders. Thank you.
The proposed rules aim to grant the European Commission enhanced powers to oversee national budgets and economic policies.
The rules will be stricter, and failing states will be imposed by Brussels technocrats with budget cuts and structural reforms in order to restore their financial balances.
This enhanced supervision is called guardianship.
States will be punished, fined, and penalized, one could say. Financial sanctions will be strengthened and more automatic.

Mr. President, dear colleagues, I am speaking on behalf of Maurice Pongard, the rapporteur for opinion of the Development Committee on Mr. Gallagher's report.
It seemed important to emphasize that, when seeking to sanction a country engaged in non-sustainable fishing practices, one must take into account its level of development.

Indeed, in the name of the principle of policy coherence with development objectives, it is crucial, in my view, that measures adopted in the field of fisheries do not undermine the development plans and strategies established by the European Union in these countries.
The draft report by Mr. Gallagher adopts this important principle for our committee on development, providing that measures must not be unjustified or even discriminatory.

I therefore urge the European Commission, when adopting such measures, to respect the principle of policy coherence with development objectives, and to duly take into account the level of development and vulnerability of countries. Thank you.
The third reform of the common fisheries policy must not once again be carried out at the expense of fishermen's interests.

No, not all fishers are these plunderers of the sea, the sole culprits behind the poor health of fish stocks, as environmental organizations and the European Commission claim when encompassing all categories of fishing.

It goes without saying that fish stocks must be preserved, but we must also enable our fishermen to earn a living from their activities and safeguard the economic fabric, vitality, and cultural identity of our coastal regions.
Indeed, the European executive's main measures once again aim at reducing the number of fishermen in order to achieve the mythical maximum sustainable yield.
I recall that the French fleet has decreased by more than fifty since 1980.

The Commission notably proposes the introduction of individual transferable quotas, seen as a guarantee of industrialization and sectoral concentration, as well as a complete ban on discharges, which professionals consider unrealistic.
This reform proposal likely suits the industrial fleet, but as it stands, would mean the programmed demise of what remains of local and artisanal fishing.

We all know here that the European Union provides more than half of the world's total public development assistance, and yet, as the 2015 deadline for the MDGs approaches, even if some goals appear to be
Affected, we must ask ourselves how to improve our assistance, making it more consistent with our trade policy, our agricultural policy, and our foreign policy.
The future of European development policy must be based on clear, measurable commitments, according to a short- and medium-term timetable.

The economic crisis, climate change, the rising power of emerging countries, and land grabbing compel us to establish new priorities—priority must be given to ensuring food security, access to energy resources, and access to healthcare.

Certainly, a European consensus exists, but we must re-examine environmental and social institutional systems at both the European and international levels, drawing inspiration from bodies that function effectively in decision-making, such as the WTO, for example.
Finally, and above all, we must explain to our fellow citizens that the development of third countries is not merely an act of charity and solidarity, but also a source of development for ourselves.
One need only look at the markets opening up for Europeans in emerging countries such as China, Brazil, and India.
2015 will also provide an opportunity to better account for the geostrategic changes of recent years, as today's emerging countries were only recently developing nations.
Now that these countries have become indispensable economic powers, they must also contribute to international aid.
The European Union must be the driving force behind responsible development assistance at both the European and international levels.

The year 2015 will be more than a deadline; it will be a unique opportunity to rethink development aid, not merely as financial assistance, but as support for responsible development initiatives, both socially and environmentally.

I therefore join my colleagues from the other groups, as well as those from the Economic and Social Committee and the Committee of the Regions, in calling for 2015 to be designated as the European Year of Development and, above all, of responsible development.
Mr. President, in my view, the Commission's agenda for change raises numerous questions, the main one being the practical implementation of the principle of differentiation.
The Commission wishes that, starting in 2014, the selection of countries in which the European Union will invest its development aid be based mechanically on the single criterion of GDP per capita.
As Ms. Ashton has reminded us, these issues are not merely accounting matters, but warrant a genuine political debate about defining our priorities in international cooperation.
Moreover, in my view, this differentiated approach is not consistent with the spirit of the Cotonou Agreement.

Mr. President, as a member of the Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection, I have closely followed the report by my colleague Regina Bastos on the twenty main concerns of citizens and businesses.
We have debated this report, in connection with our broader discussion on the twenty years of the internal market.

It has already been twenty years since the free movement of goods, people, services, and capital has been deemed capable of being fully exercised within our space of life, exchange, and internal market.

I was particularly struck by the fact that, at the forefront, the concerns expressed by our European fellow citizens relate to the absence of professional qualifications, and that we must therefore be truly proactive and highly effective.

This is what our fellow citizens expect—truly achieving, in the very near future, procedures for recognizing their qualifications, skills, and professional credentials that are significantly faster, simpler, more effective, and less costly.
It is a genuine challenge.

As the fictive rapporteur for the EPE group on the subject of modernizing these procedures, I am actively working with the other members of the IMCO Committee to truly address this major challenge to mobility.
Greater mobility, for a greater Europe—this is what our fellow citizens are asking for. Thank you.

It is, for me, the clear signal of the year 2011 for this Commission.

Citizens already had a body to which they could turn in the event of a violation of their rights, but it is now also the case that they may address this body when they have proposals for improving European legislation.
I wish that the Petitions Committee could thus increasingly be seen as the preferred point of contact for citizens within the European Parliament.
This issue of consideration is also found in the findings of the petitions committee's hearings.
